User.title: User
User.description: Represents a user of the Interest Hub application.

createdAt.description: Timestamp indicating when the user account was created.
Timestamp indicating when the interest was created.

Topic.title: Topic
Topic.description: Represents a topic within an interest.

createdAt.description: Timestamp indicating when the topic was created.

Goal.title: Goal
Goal.description: Represents a goal within a topic.

dueDate.description: The date and time when the goal is due.

isCompleted.description: Indicates whether the goal has been completed.

createdAt.description: Timestamp indicating when the goal was created.

Task.title: Task
Task.description: Represents a task associated with a goal.

dueDate.description: The date and time when the task is due.

isCompleted.description: Indicates whether the task has been completed.

createdAt.description: Timestamp indicating when the task was created.

firestore.reasoning:
The Firestore data structure is designed to support the Interest Hub application's features, focusing on user interests, topics, goals, and tasks. The structure employs path-based ownership for data privacy and easy management, aligning with the core design principles of Authorization Independence and Structural Segregation. Specifically, each user has a dedicated space for their interests, topics, goals and tasks. This enables efficient security rules based on the `request.auth.uid`. This design avoids complex authorization logic and simplifies security rules.

The chosen structure facilitates straightforward queries. For instance, retrieving all interests for a user involves querying the `/users/{userId}/interests` collection. Similarly, tasks associated with a goal can be efficiently fetched from `/users/{userId}/interests/{interestId}/topics/{topicId}/goals/{goalId}/tasks`. This hierarchical organization ensures that each request is scoped to a specific user and their data.

To enforce Authorization Independence, the `userId` is included in each `Interest` document. This ensures that access control to an interest can be determined solely based on the interest document itself without needing to `get()` the parent user document. This pattern extends to `Topic`, `Goal`, and `Task` entities. For example, the `Topic` entity includes the `interestId` which is nested under a `userId`. The nesting continues for the `Goal` which includes a `topicId` and the `Task` entity includes a `goalId`. This creates a complete hierarchy for each entity which can be created atomically without `get()` calls in the security rules.

Regarding QAPs (Rules are not Filters), the structure segregates data based on ownership. Listing operations are inherently secure because each user can only access their own data via the path-based structure. This eliminates the need for filtering based on user ID within the rules, as the path itself ensures the correct scope. In other words, the security rules are not filters.
